---
title: "Extensions"
sidebar: tips-sidebar
bibliography: ../../Qmd Files/Example Page/references.bib
filters:
  - parmsam/quizdown
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
  - py-exercise
  - jsxgraph/jsxgraph
---

This chapter covers the four extensions used in this tutorial.
Each extension is installed once per project with `quarto add <name>` — the files land in `_extensions/` and should be committed to git.

Extensions can be enabled **globally** in `_quarto.yml` or **per page** in the frontmatter:

```{.yaml filename="_quarto.yml — global"}
filters:
  - parmsam/quizdown
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
```

```{.yaml filename="page.qmd — per page"}
---
filters:
  - parmsam/quizdown
---
```

---

## quizdown — interactive quizzes

**Install:** `quarto add parmsam/quizdown`
**Source:** [@quizdown] — [github.com/parmsam/quarto-quizdown](https://github.com/parmsam/quarto-quizdown)

:::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
```{quizdown}
# Which file controls global project settings in Quarto?

1. [x] `_quarto.yml`
2. [ ] `index.qmd`
3. [ ] `styles.css`

# Select all valid Quarto output formats.

1. [x] HTML
2. [x] PDF
3. [x] Word (.docx)
4. [ ] Excel (.xlsx)

# Put the Quarto render pipeline in the correct order.

1. Parse `.qmd` source
2. Execute code chunks
3. Convert Markdown to HTML/PDF
4. Apply theme and styles
```
::::

:::: {.content-hidden when-format="html"}
::: {.callout-note}
## Interactive quiz — HTML only
This quiz requires HTML output. Render as HTML to try it.
:::
::::

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Settings

Global quiz settings go inside a `---` block at the top of the quiz:

````{.markdown}
```{quizdown}
---
shuffleAnswers: true    # default: true — randomise answer order
shuffleQuestions: false # default: false — randomise question order
---
```
````

::: {.callout-tip}
`shuffleAnswers: true` is the **default** — answers are shuffled unless you explicitly set it to `false`.
:::

## Question types

**Single choice** — exactly one correct answer:
````{.markdown}
```{quizdown}
# What is 2 + 2?
1. [ ] 3
2. [x] 4
3. [ ] 5
```
````

**Multiple choice** — more than one correct answer:
````{.markdown}
```{quizdown}
# Which of these are programming languages?
1. [x] Python
2. [ ] Quarto
3. [x] R
4. [x] JavaScript
```
````

## Images & code

Include an image in a question:
````{.markdown}
```{quizdown}
# What does this logo belong to?
![](https://quarto.org/quarto.png)
1. [x] Quarto
2. [ ] RMarkdown
```
````

Include inline code in answers:
````{.markdown}
```{quizdown}
# Which chunk option hides the source code?
1. [x] `echo: false`
2. [ ] `eval: false`
3. [ ] `output: false`
```
````

:::

---

## Pyodide — live Python in the browser

**Install:** `quarto add coatless-quarto/pyodide`
**Source:** [@pyodide] — [github.com/coatless-quarto/pyodide](https://github.com/coatless-quarto/pyodide)

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Example

:::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
```{pyodide-python}
# Edit this code and click Run
words = ["Quarto", "is", "great"]
print(" ".join(words))
```
::::

:::: {.content-hidden when-format="html"}
::: {.callout-note}
## Interactive code cell — HTML only
This live Python cell runs in the browser via Pyodide. Render as HTML to try it.
:::
::::

## Setup & options

Enable per page:
```{.yaml}
---
filters:
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
---
```

Or globally in `_quarto.yml` (applies to all pages):
```{.yaml}
filters:
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
```

Per-chunk options:
````{.markdown}
```{pyodide-python}
#| autorun: true     # run automatically on page load
#| readonly: true    # reader cannot edit the code
print("Auto-runs, cannot be changed.")
```
````

## vs. regular Python

| | `{python}` | `{pyodide-python}` |
|---|---|---|
| Runs at | Render time | Read time (in browser) |
| Reader can edit | No | Yes |
| Needs Python installed | Yes (author) | No (reader) |
| Output baked into HTML | Yes | No — computed live |

::: {.callout-warning}
## Package availability
Pyodide runs in WebAssembly. Most pure-Python packages work (`numpy`, `pandas`, `matplotlib`) but packages with C extensions may not be available.
:::

:::

---

## py-exercise — editable exercises with hidden tests

**Install:** `quarto add Erasmus-CTM/py-exercise`
**Source:** [@py-exercise] — [github.com/Erasmus-CTM/py-exercise](https://github.com/Erasmus-CTM/py-exercise)

::: {.callout-important}
## Requires Pyodide
py-exercise runs student code in the browser using Pyodide.
**Both filters must be active on the same page** — either globally in `_quarto.yml` or together in the page frontmatter:
```{.yaml}
filters:
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
  - py-exercise
```
:::


::: {.panel-tabset}

## Example

:::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
```{py-exercise}
def square(x):
    """Return x squared."""
    # Your solution here
    pass

## TESTS ##
assert square(3) == 9,  "square(3) should be 9"
assert square(0) == 0,  "square(0) should be 0"
assert square(-2) == 4, "square(-2) should be 4"
```
::::

:::: {.content-hidden when-format="html"}
::: {.callout-note}
## Interactive exercise — HTML only
This exercise runs student code in the browser. Render as HTML to try it.
:::
::::

## Syntax

````{.markdown}
```{py-exercise}
def my_function(x):
    """Docstring shown to the reader."""
    # Student fills this in
    pass

## TESTS ##
assert my_function(2) == 4, "Feedback shown on failure"
assert my_function(0) == 0
```
````

Rules:
1. Write the function stub with a docstring at the top.
2. `## TESTS ##` is the separator — everything below is hidden from the reader.
3. Each `assert` statement's message becomes the feedback shown on test failure.

::: {.callout-tip}
Write assertion messages as if you are talking to the student:
`"my_function(2) should return 4 — did you multiply correctly?"`
:::

:::

---

## JSXGraph — interactive mathematical graphs

**Install:** `quarto add jsxgraph/jsxgraph`
**Source:** [@jsxgraph] — [github.com/jsxgraph/jsxgraph-quarto](https://github.com/jsxgraph/jsxgraph-quarto)

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Example

:::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
```jsxgraph
var board = JXG.JSXGraph.initBoard(BOARDID, {
    boundingbox: [-7, 1.8, 7, -1.8],
    axis: true,
    showNavigation: false
});
board.create('functiongraph', [Math.sin], {
    strokeColor: '#0d6efd', strokeWidth: 2.5
});
var p = board.create('glider', [1.57, 0, board.defaultAxes.x], {
    name: 'x', size: 5, fillColor: '#E91E63'
});
board.create('point', [
    function() { return p.X(); },
    function() { return Math.sin(p.X()); }
], { name: function() { return 'sin(x) ≈ ' + Math.sin(p.X()).toFixed(3); },
     size: 5, fillColor: '#198754', fixed: true });
```
::::

:::: {.content-hidden when-format="html"}
::: {.callout-note}
## Interactive graph — HTML only
This JSXGraph canvas is rendered in the browser. Render as HTML to interact with it.
:::
::::

## Syntax

````{.markdown}
```jsxgraph
var board = JXG.JSXGraph.initBoard(BOARDID, {
    boundingbox: [-5, 5, 5, -5],   // [xmin, ymax, xmax, ymin]
    axis: true,
    keepaspectratio: false,
    showNavigation: false
});
```
````

::: {.callout-important}
Always use the literal string `BOARDID` — Quarto replaces it with a unique ID.
Hardcoding a string breaks pages with multiple graphs.
:::

## Common elements

```{.javascript}
// Function graph
board.create('functiongraph', [Math.sin], { strokeColor: '#0d6efd' });

// Draggable point
var p = board.create('point', [2, 1], { name: 'P', size: 4 });

// Glider — point constrained to a line/curve
var g = board.create('glider', [1, 0, board.defaultAxes.x], { name: 'x' });

// Slider
var s = board.create('slider', [[0,-3],[4,-3],[0,1,2*Math.PI]], { name: 'a' });

// Use slider value
board.create('functiongraph', [function(x) { return Math.sin(s.Value()*x); }]);

// Text label
board.create('text', [1, 2, function() { return 'x = ' + g.X().toFixed(2); }]);

// Segment
board.create('segment', [p1, p2], { strokeColor: '#198754', dash: 2 });
```

:::
